title: JSON.stringify — object to string
instruction:
`JSON.stringify(value)` converts a JavaScript value to a JSON string.

Notice what gets LOST:
- `undefined` values are omitted
- Functions are omitted
- `Date` objects become strings
- `Symbol` keys are omitted

Run this.
successMessage: undefined, functions, and Symbols are silently dropped. Date becomes an ISO string. null stays as null. These are the JSON gotchas that bite everyone at least once.

title: JSON.stringify with indent
instruction:
The second and third arguments to `JSON.stringify`:
- `replacer` — filter/transform what gets serialized (null = keep all)
- `indent` — number of spaces for pretty-printing

`JSON.stringify(data, null, 2)` — pretty print with 2-space indent.
successMessage: null, 2 — keep all fields, indent 2 spaces. Use compact for APIs (save bandwidth), pretty-print for logs and config files.

title: JSON.parse — string to object
instruction:
`JSON.parse(str)` converts a JSON string back to a JavaScript value.

Always wrap in `try/catch` — malformed JSON throws a `SyntaxError`.

Parse valid and invalid JSON and handle both cases.
successMessage: Always try/catch around JSON.parse. User-supplied JSON, API responses, localStorage values — all can be malformed. safeJsonParse is a pattern worth keeping.

title: Replacer — filter and transform
instruction:
The `replacer` argument controls what gets serialized:

- Array of keys: only include those keys
- Function `(key, value) => newValue`: transform each value

Remove passwords and transform dates.
hint: replacer function: if key === 'password' return undefined
successMessage: Array replacer: whitelist. Function replacer: control each field. Return undefined to omit. Return a new value to transform. Never send passwords to the client — use replacer to strip them.

title: Reviver — transform on parse
instruction:
`JSON.parse(str, reviver)` lets you transform values while parsing.

Most common use: convert ISO date strings back to `Date` objects (since JSON has no native Date type).
successMessage: Reviver restores Date objects from ISO strings. Without it, every date field is a string and you have to convert manually. JSON serialization libraries like superjson do this automatically.

title: Custom toJSON method
instruction:
When a class has a `toJSON()` method, `JSON.stringify` uses its return value instead of the raw object.

This controls exactly how your class serializes.
successMessage: toJSON() is called automatically by JSON.stringify. Private fields stay private. Dates are formatted. This is the clean way to control serialization of your classes.

title: Handling circular references
instruction:
Objects that reference themselves crash `JSON.stringify` with `TypeError: circular structure`.

Detect and handle circular references with a custom replacer.
successMessage: Circular references — common in tree structures, linked lists, and object graphs. safeStringify handles them gracefully. Libraries like json-stringify-safe do this in production.

title: Build a data serialiser
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a `Serialiser` class with:
- `serialise(data)` — converts to JSON string, handles Dates as ISO strings, strips undefined
- `deserialise(str)` — parses JSON, converts ISO date strings back to Date objects
- `serialiseFile(data, indent)` — pretty-printed (for config files)

Test with an object containing nested objects, arrays, Dates, and undefined values.
hint: Use replacer in stringify and reviver in parse. DATE_PATTERN = /^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}T/
successMessage: A complete serialisation library with Date handling, custom replacer, and reviver. You have now covered everything in JavaScript from console.log to async generators to JSON. Chapter 22 done — JavaScript complete!
